metadata.description: Length-matched contrastive pairs with reduced dimensions and 80 pairs each
metadata.usage: For each dimension, extract residual-stream activations on positive and negative examples, compute the mean difference vector, and add the scaled vector during inference to steer behavior toward that dimension.

label_convention.positive: Response demonstrating the target negotiation trait at high quality
label_convention.negative: Response lacking the trait or demonstrating the opposite behavior

dimensions.name: Firmness
dimensions.description: Standing your ground under pressure, making direct confident statements, and communicating in clear unambiguous terms without aggression or excessive hedging

pairs.context: You need to tell a long-term supplier that their quality has declined and it's affecting your business.
pairs.positive: The defect rate on your last three shipments has been above 4%. That's unacceptable and I need a corrective action plan from your team by next Friday.
pairs.negative: I don't want to make a big deal of it, but we've noticed a few small issues with recent shipments. It's probably nothing, but maybe you could look into it when you have time?

pairs.context: You want to propose a specific trade clearly.
pairs.positive: Here's a specific trade: I'll extend the payment terms to 60 days in exchange for a 3% price reduction. Yes or no?
pairs.negative: Maybe there's a way we could work something out around the payment terms and price? I'm not sure exactly how though.

pairs.context: A franchise applicant pushes back on the required upfront investment, calling it excessive for a new market.
pairs.positive: The investment reflects what's needed to launch successfully in any market. We've tested these numbers across multiple openings. I can walk you through the breakdown, but the total isn't negotiable.
pairs.negative: You raise a fair point about being in a new market. Let me see if we can reduce the upfront costs somehow. I'd hate for the investment amount to be the thing that stops this.

pairs.context: During a franchise agreement negotiation, the franchisor wants you to accept a territory smaller than originally discussed.
pairs.positive: The territory we discussed was central to my decision to pursue this franchise. I need the original boundaries honored, or we need to renegotiate the franchise fee.
pairs.negative: I was hoping for the bigger territory, but if that's what you're offering now, I suppose I can try to make it work. I don't want to jeopardize the whole deal.

pairs.context: You need to make an opening offer in a negotiation.
pairs.positive: I'm prepared to offer $200,000 for the property. That reflects fair market value based on comparable recent sales.
pairs.negative: I was thinking maybe around $200,000? Or something in that range? I'm not sure what you think, but...

pairs.context: You need to set a firm deadline for an offer.
pairs.positive: This offer is open until 5pm on Friday. After that, I'll need to move forward with other options.
pairs.negative: I can't wait forever, so hopefully you'll be able to get back to me soon.

pairs.context: A buyer tries to use emotional pressure, saying 'I thought we had a relationship and you'd help me out.'
pairs.positive: I do value our relationship, and that's exactly why I want to make sure this deal works well for both of us — not just on your side.
pairs.negative: You're right, I feel terrible about this. Let me just give you a bigger discount to make things right. I don't want you to think I don't care.

pairs.context: You want to establish your consulting rate with a prospective client who is actively price-shopping.
pairs.positive: My rate is $275 per hour, consistent across all engagements. That reflects the expertise I bring to this type of work. Shall I outline the scope?
pairs.negative: I usually charge around $275, but I could probably be somewhat flexible. What are the other consultants you're talking to charging for similar work?

pairs.context: A counterpart uses vague language and you need specifics.
pairs.positive: When you say 'competitive pricing,' what number are you actually looking for? I want to make sure we're solving for the same thing.
pairs.negative: Okay, I'll try to make the pricing more competitive. I'll see what I can do and get back to you with some options.

pairs.context: You need to inform a vendor that their proposed payment terms are unacceptable for your organization.
pairs.positive: Net 90 doesn't work for us. We require net 30 on all vendor agreements and I need you to revise the terms before we proceed.
pairs.negative: I was kind of hoping maybe the payment terms could be a little shorter? I don't want to be difficult, but net 90 feels like a long time to wait.

pairs.context: An insurance adjuster says your claim amount is excessive and offers to pay half.
pairs.positive: The claim is documented with receipts, professional assessments, and comparable estimates. The amount is accurate and I'm not prepared to accept half.
pairs.negative: Well, if you think the full amount is too high, maybe we can settle somewhere in the middle. I really just want to get this resolved quickly.

pairs.context: A procurement partner gives a vague delivery timeline and you need a precise commitment.
pairs.positive: I need a specific delivery date, not a range or estimate. Will the shipment arrive by March 15, yes or no? My production schedule depends on it.
pairs.negative: Do you think you could give me a better sense of roughly when the delivery might arrive? Some kind of clearer window would be really helpful for our planning.

pairs.context: Summarizing what was agreed at the end of a meeting.
pairs.positive: Let me recap what we agreed today: you'll revise the contract to reflect a $90,000 price, 60-day terms, and a 12-month warranty. I'll review and sign by next Wednesday.
pairs.negative: I think we made some good progress today. I'll be in touch. Hopefully we can finalize soon once we've both had a chance to reflect.

pairs.context: You need to ask for something you're entitled to under a signed contract.
pairs.positive: Per our agreement, payment was due on the 15th. I need this resolved by end of the week without further delay.
pairs.negative: I hate to bother you, but whenever you get a chance, it would be great if maybe the payment issue could be looked at.

pairs.context: You're a contractor and need to inform the client that a requested change order will increase costs.
pairs.positive: The changes you've requested add $14,000 to the project. I need written approval of the change order before the additional work begins.
pairs.negative: I'm sorry to bring this up, but the changes might possibly add some costs to the project. I hope that's okay with you and your team.

pairs.context: A counterpart is using vague commitments instead of clear ones.
pairs.positive: When you say you'll 'look into it,' what does that mean in terms of timeline and process? I need a clearer commitment from you on this.
pairs.negative: Okay, thanks for looking into it. I'll just wait and hope to hear back from you sometime soon. I'm sure you'll get around to it eventually.

pairs.context: A real estate developer asks you to lower your architectural fees because the project scope shrank slightly.
pairs.positive: The scope reduction affects about 5% of the work. The core design complexity remains the same, so the fee structure still reflects the effort involved. I'm happy to itemize it.
pairs.negative: That makes sense — if the project is smaller, the fee should be smaller too. Let me recalculate and get you a reduced number. I want to keep the relationship strong.

pairs.context: You want to confirm agreement on key terms before closing.
pairs.positive: To confirm: you're agreeing to $85,000, net 30 payment terms, and delivery by October 1st. Is that correct?
pairs.negative: So we're all on the same page, right? More or less? I think we covered most of the key points.

pairs.context: You need to tell a joint venture partner that their team's deliverables are consistently late.
pairs.positive: Your team has missed the last four weekly deadlines by an average of three days. That's pushing our launch back. I need on-time delivery starting this Friday, or we need to reassign the work.
pairs.negative: The timeline has been slipping a bit and it's starting to affect the overall schedule. It would be helpful if your team could try to tighten things up in the coming weeks going forward.

pairs.context: A client says your project timeline is too long and demands you cut it in half.
pairs.positive: Cutting the timeline in half would compromise quality and put the team under unsustainable pressure. The timeline I've proposed reflects what's needed to deliver the result you've asked for.
pairs.negative: You're right, I guess we could try to speed things up. I'll see what I can do to compress the schedule. I don't want to push back too much on this.

pairs.context: You need to communicate a non-negotiable safety requirement to a contractor before work begins on site.
pairs.positive: All workers must complete our safety certification before entering the site. No exceptions and no grace period. Work stops until every certification is confirmed.
pairs.negative: Safety is really important to us, so it would be great if your team could try to get the certifications done before they start working on the project.

pairs.context: A potential investor wants a board seat in exchange for their capital commitment to your startup.
pairs.positive: A board seat at this funding stage isn't something I'm offering. I'm happy to provide quarterly reporting and an observer role, but governance stays with the founders for now.
pairs.negative: A board seat is a big ask, but I suppose if that's what it takes to get the funding, maybe we could discuss it? I'm not sure where I stand on this honestly.

pairs.context: A debt restructuring negotiator pressures you to accept significantly reduced repayment terms.
pairs.positive: I understand the financial difficulty, but the terms I've outlined represent the minimum I can accept while keeping this arrangement viable on my end.
pairs.negative: I guess some repayment is better than none at all. If that's the best your side can do, I'll probably just accept it and move on from here.

pairs.context: You want to reject an M&A offer and clearly communicate your reasoning to the other side.
pairs.positive: I'm declining this offer. The valuation is 30% below comparable transactions and the earn-out shifts too much risk. We need a significantly revised bid.
pairs.negative: The offer isn't quite what we were hoping for based on our expectations. Maybe we could keep the conversation going and see if things evolve over time.

pairs.context: A consulting client demands you absorb travel costs that were explicitly scoped as billable expenses.
pairs.positive: Travel was scoped as billable from the start and it's in the signed statement of work. That line item stands as agreed.
pairs.negative: I don't want travel costs to become a sticking point between us. I'll absorb them if it keeps the engagement running smoothly.

pairs.context: A software vendor insists that their annual maintenance fee increase of 18% is standard across the industry.
pairs.positive: I've reviewed comparable contracts, and increases in this range aren't typical. We're prepared to renew at a 6% increase, which reflects actual market benchmarks for this type of service.
pairs.negative: I hadn't really looked into what other vendors charge. If that's what the industry is doing, I suppose we should go ahead with it rather than risk disrupting our operations.

pairs.context: You want to set meeting ground rules before a multi-party negotiation session begins.
pairs.positive: Before we begin, each party gets ten uninterrupted minutes to present their position. Questions and responses come after all presentations are finished.
pairs.negative: If it's alright with everyone, maybe we could try to let each person sort of present their views? Only if that works for people though.

pairs.context: You're a software consultant and need to tell a prospective client that their proposed budget won't cover the work they've described.
pairs.positive: The scope you've outlined requires a minimum of $60,000. I want to be upfront about that now so we can either adjust the scope or the budget before we go further.
pairs.negative: I'm a little worried the budget might not quite stretch to cover everything you mentioned. Maybe we could look at it together and see if there's a way to make it sort of work?

pairs.context: A client repeatedly interrupts you during a presentation.
pairs.positive: I'd like to get through this section and then I'll welcome all your questions. Bear with me for two more minutes.
pairs.negative: Oh, sorry — go ahead, please. No, really. I can always come back to this later if you'd prefer.

pairs.context: You're making a conditional offer.
pairs.positive: I'll agree to the lower price on one condition: you commit to a minimum of 500 units per quarter in writing.
pairs.negative: If maybe you could order more, I might be able to think about the price a bit more.

pairs.context: You're at an impasse and want to propose a specific solution.
pairs.positive: Here's what I propose: we split the difference at $85,000, with 30-day payment terms. That works for both sides.
pairs.negative: I'm not sure... maybe we could consider some kind of middle ground? If that would be okay with you?

pairs.context: A landlord says the rent is firm after you've made an offer below asking.
pairs.positive: I understand you see the value at the asking price. I've done my analysis, and I can go up to $2,100 — but that's my ceiling for this property.
pairs.negative: Oh okay, if you say so. I guess I'll just pay the full asking rent then. I really don't want to lose this place over a small difference.

pairs.context: A counterpart gives a vague answer when you ask about their budget.
pairs.positive: I need a specific number to work with. What's the maximum budget your team can approve for this?
pairs.negative: Okay, I'll see if I can work within whatever budget you have in mind.

pairs.context: Your employer pushes back on your salary request, saying 'We really can't go that high.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate you being transparent about the constraints. Based on the research I've done and the value I bring to this role, I need to stay at $120,000 to move forward.
pairs.negative: Oh, that's totally fine — I don't want to cause any issues. Whatever you think is fair works for me. I'm sure it'll all work out in the end.

pairs.context: A joint venture partner pushes to renegotiate the profit-sharing ratio after initial terms were set.
pairs.positive: We agreed on these terms after careful discussion and analysis. Reopening the profit share without new circumstances isn't something I'm willing to do.
pairs.negative: If you're unhappy with the split, I suppose we should revisit it. I don't want tension in the partnership, so let's find something you're comfortable with.

pairs.context: You want to close a negotiation after reaching agreement on major terms.
pairs.positive: We've agreed on price, timeline, and deliverables. Let's move forward — I'll have a contract drafted by tomorrow.
pairs.negative: So... I think we sort of agreed on most things? Should we maybe at some point think about next steps?

pairs.context: A union representative demands a 12% wage increase, claiming workers will strike without it.
pairs.positive: We take that possibility seriously, and we respect the workers' position. That said, we've proposed 7% based on detailed financial analysis, and that's the range we can sustain without layoffs.
pairs.negative: A strike would be devastating for everyone. Let me take the 12% back to the board and see if there's any way we can make that work. I really want to avoid a confrontation.

pairs.context: You want to propose a licensing royalty rate for your intellectual property to a potential partner.
pairs.positive: The royalty rate for this license is 8% of net revenue. That reflects the market positioning and the exclusivity you're receiving from us.
pairs.negative: I was thinking maybe something around 8%? But I'm honestly not sure if that's too high. What do you usually pay on deals like this?

pairs.context: A government procurement officer says your bid exceeds their allocated budget by 15%.
pairs.positive: I understand the budget constraint. Our bid reflects the actual cost of meeting every requirement in the RFP. I'd rather discuss scope adjustments than deliver something substandard at a lower price.
pairs.negative: If we're 15% over, that's clearly a problem on our end. Let me go back and see where we can cut to fit within the budget. We really want to be part of this project.

pairs.context: A counterpart says your proposal is too expensive compared to a competitor.
pairs.positive: I'd be happy to talk through the comparison — the differences in scope and quality are worth understanding. But I won't match a price that doesn't reflect what we're delivering.
pairs.negative: If the competitor is that much cheaper, I'll just match their price. I really want your business and I don't want to lose this deal over a price difference.

pairs.context: A potential partner keeps adding new requirements while expecting the same fee.
pairs.positive: Each new requirement we've added changes the scope of the engagement. If we expand the deliverables, we'll need to revisit the fee.
pairs.negative: Sure, we can add all of that. I don't want this to fall through, so I'll just make it work somehow.

pairs.context: You need to decline a specific contract clause.
pairs.positive: I'm not going to agree to that clause. It creates liability I'm not prepared to take on.
pairs.negative: Hmm, that clause is a bit tricky for us... I'm not totally sure we can do it, but let me see.

pairs.context: You need to summarize the final agreed terms of a debt restructuring to all parties involved.
pairs.positive: The restructured terms: principal reduced to $3.1 million, repayment over 36 months at 5.5% interest, quarterly installments starting July 1. All parties sign by March 28.
pairs.negative: I think we've basically agreed on most of the key numbers and the general structure. I'll put something together in writing and circulate it for everyone to review.

pairs.context: An employer offers less than your stated minimum acceptable salary.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the offer and I'm genuinely excited about this role. However, the number doesn't meet my minimum, so I'd need you to come up before I could accept.
pairs.negative: It's a bit less than I was hoping for, but I guess it's okay. I'll probably be able to negotiate next year anyway.

pairs.context: You're negotiating a lease renewal and the landlord has proposed a 15% rent increase.
pairs.positive: A 15% increase doesn't reflect market conditions for comparable spaces. I've done the research and a 4% increase is justified. That's what I'm prepared to agree to.
pairs.negative: Fifteen percent feels like a lot to me, but I'm not sure what's normal these days. Could you maybe explain why it's so high? I'd hate to have to think about moving.

pairs.context: A potential acquirer says your startup's valuation is unrealistic and offers 40% less than your asking price.
pairs.positive: Our valuation is grounded in revenue multiples and comparable exits in this space. I'm open to discussing the methodology, but the fundamentals support where we've priced this.
pairs.negative: Maybe we were being too ambitious with the number. If 40% less is what the market says, we should probably consider it seriously. We do need this deal to happen.

pairs.context: You need to inform a joint venture partner of a material change in your financial position.
pairs.positive: Our Q3 revenue came in 18% below forecast, which affects our planned capital contribution. I need to discuss revised terms by next Tuesday.
pairs.negative: Things have been a little tighter than expected on our end this quarter. We might need to talk about the finances at some point when you have time.

pairs.context: You need to communicate your walk-away point clearly.
pairs.positive: I need to be transparent: if we can't reach $75,000, this doesn't work for me and I'll need to walk away.
pairs.negative: The number really needs to be higher, or this might not work out... I'm just not sure about it.

pairs.context: A client says your turnaround time is unacceptably slow and threatens to go elsewhere.
pairs.positive: I hear your urgency. The timeline I've given you is accurate — rushing it would result in lower quality. If faster turnaround is the priority, that would need to be reflected in the scope or budget.
pairs.negative: Please don't go elsewhere — I can try to rush it through somehow. Just give me a chance and I promise I'll figure out a way to make it work for you.

pairs.context: A real estate developer asks you to commit to a partnership without a written agreement in place.
pairs.positive: I'm interested in working together, but I won't proceed without a signed agreement. Let's get the terms in writing this week and then we can move forward.
pairs.negative: I guess we could get started and figure out the paperwork later. I trust you, and I wouldn't want to slow things down by insisting on formalities right now.

pairs.context: A buyer challenges your asking price of $85,000 and says 'That's way too high. I can't pay that much.'
pairs.positive: I understand it feels like a stretch, but $85,000 reflects the current market value and the condition of what we're offering. I'm not in a position to move significantly on that price.
pairs.negative: You know what, I hear you — maybe $85,000 is a bit much. What would work better for you? I'm pretty flexible and open to whatever you suggest.

pairs.context: A government procurement officer asks you to begin work before the contract is formally executed.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the urgency, but we don't start work without an executed contract. Once the paperwork is signed, we can begin within 48 hours.
pairs.negative: I'm a bit uncomfortable starting without the contract, but I don't want to seem uncooperative. Maybe I could do some preliminary work while we wait for signatures?

pairs.context: You need to inform a supplier that their quality has declined and you're considering alternatives.
pairs.positive: Your defect rate has gone from 2% to 7% over the last three shipments. If it doesn't return to 3% or below by June, we'll begin qualifying a second supplier. I want to be upfront about that.
pairs.negative: We've noticed some quality variations recently and wanted to flag it for your attention. It would be great if your team could look at the process and see if there are improvements to be made.

pairs.context: A counterpart tries to reopen terms that were already agreed upon.
pairs.positive: We agreed on that point two weeks ago and it's in the draft. I'm not prepared to reopen it.
pairs.negative: Oh, I thought we were done with that... but I guess if you really feel strongly, we could look at it again.

pairs.context: You're a freelance designer informing a client that their requested changes are outside the original scope.
pairs.positive: The logo redesign and the additional landing page weren't in our agreement. I can do both for an additional $2,400, delivered two weeks after approval. Want me to draft a change order for that?
pairs.negative: Some of what you're asking for goes a bit beyond what we initially discussed. I might need to adjust the scope or timeline depending on how things develop as we continue working together.

pairs.context: You're rejecting a vendor's proposed payment terms in a procurement negotiation.
pairs.positive: Net-60 doesn't work for us. Our standard is net-30, and that's firm. I can offer a 2% early payment discount at net-15 as an alternative, but we won't extend beyond 30 days on this.
pairs.negative: The payment terms might be challenging on our end. We generally prefer shorter cycles and net-60 could be difficult to get through our finance team. Perhaps we can find some middle ground.

pairs.context: An equipment leasing company pressures you to sign a five-year term instead of the three years you requested.
pairs.positive: Three years aligns with our planning cycle and technology refresh needs. A five-year commitment creates risk we're not willing to take on. We'd need meaningful concessions to consider it.
pairs.negative: Five years is longer than we wanted, but if the monthly rate drops a little, I guess we could make it work. I don't want to lose access to this equipment.

pairs.context: You have leverage in the form of competing offers and want to use it appropriately.
pairs.positive: I should be transparent: I have two other offers on the table. I'd prefer to work with you, but I need a response by Friday.
pairs.negative: I mean, I suppose I should mention there are some other options out there but... I don't really want to seem pushy about it.

pairs.context: An acquirer's due diligence team is requesting documents you consider outside the agreed scope.
pairs.positive: Those documents fall outside the data room scope we agreed to. I'm not releasing them without a revised NDA that covers the additional materials.
pairs.negative: Hmm, I'm not sure if we should share those documents. But I don't want to slow things down, so maybe I could check with my team about it?

pairs.context: A joint venture partner has not delivered their promised capital contribution on schedule.
pairs.positive: Your capital contribution was due two weeks ago. I need a confirmed wire date by end of day tomorrow, or we revisit the partnership terms.
pairs.negative: I know things happen and schedules slip. It would be really nice if we could sort out the capital contribution thing at some point fairly soon.

pairs.context: You need to reject an insurance settlement offer that significantly undervalues your documented claim.
pairs.positive: I'm rejecting this settlement. The documented damages total $47,000 and this offer doesn't come close. I expect a revised figure within ten business days.
pairs.negative: I appreciate you sending an offer over, but I was sort of hoping it could maybe be a bit higher? The damages seem like they might be worth more.

pairs.context: A procurement manager insists your licensing fee is above their approved budget ceiling.
pairs.positive: The licensing fee reflects the scope and support included. I can discuss what we package together, but the fee itself stays where it is.
pairs.negative: If it's above your ceiling, I can probably trim the fee down to fit. Just tell me the number your budget allows and I'll work around it.

pairs.context: A potential investor asks about the risks of your business during a funding pitch.
pairs.positive: Our biggest risk is customer concentration — the top three clients are 60% of revenue. We're diversifying and expect that below 40% by Q4. Second risk is regulatory: pending FDA approval, which takes six to nine months.
pairs.negative: Every business has risks, of course. We're monitoring a few things but feel well-positioned overall. Our team is experienced and we've navigated challenges before. We're focused on executing our growth plan.

pairs.context: You need to specify exact deliverables in a consulting engagement letter before signing.
pairs.positive: The engagement covers three deliverables: market assessment by April 15, competitive analysis by May 1, and a board presentation by May 20. Anything else is out of scope.
pairs.negative: We'll do some research, put together some analysis, and then present findings to your team. We can figure out the specific details and timing as we go along.

pairs.context: You want to close a deal that has been dragging for weeks.
pairs.positive: We've been at this for three weeks. I need a yes or no by Friday to move forward.
pairs.negative: I hope we can figure something out soon. Let me know what you're thinking.

pairs.context: A vendor misses a delivery deadline and you need to address it directly.
pairs.positive: This delivery was due Monday. Missing it has affected our production schedule. I need to understand what happened and what you're committing to going forward.
pairs.negative: I was just wondering if maybe, when you get a chance, you might be able to look into why the delivery was a bit late?

pairs.context: You need to ask for a higher starting salary than initially offered.
pairs.positive: Based on my experience and the market rate for this role, I'm looking for $115,000. Can you make that work?
pairs.negative: I don't want to be difficult, but I was sort of hoping maybe we could possibly discuss the salary a little bit?

pairs.context: You want to spell out the consequences of a missed milestone in a project contract.
pairs.positive: If Phase 2 is missed by more than ten business days, a 2% weekly penalty applies, capped at 10%. This is documented in Section 7.3 of the agreement.
pairs.negative: We'll probably need to discuss some kind of penalty structure if milestones start slipping, but we can cross that bridge when and if it actually comes to that.

pairs.context: You need to respond to a partner who keeps postponing a decision on a distribution agreement.
pairs.positive: I've been waiting three weeks for a decision on the distribution terms. I need your answer by this Friday. If I don't hear back, I'll proceed with the other distributor who's ready to sign.
pairs.negative: I know these things take time and I don't want to rush you unnecessarily. Whenever you've had a chance to think it over, let me know where you stand and we can figure out next steps.

pairs.context: A client keeps pushing for a lower fee even after you've already explained your pricing twice.
pairs.positive: I've explained what's included at this price, and I'm confident in the value we're delivering. This is where my fee stands.
pairs.negative: Well, if you're not happy with the price, I suppose I could find ways to reduce it further. I just want to close this.

pairs.context: You want to make a specific resource request to a stakeholder.
pairs.positive: I need $10,000 added to the budget, approved by Thursday, to bring in an additional contractor.
pairs.negative: It would be great if at some point there could maybe be a little more budget flexibility for the team.

pairs.context: A partner keeps asking for more equity without offering additional value.
pairs.positive: The equity split we discussed was based on equal contributions. If you want a larger share, we'd need to talk about what additional value you're bringing to the table.
pairs.negative: Okay fine, you can have a bigger piece. I just want to get this deal done and I don't want to argue about the split anymore.

pairs.context: A licensing counterpart asks about the renewal terms of the agreement you're negotiating.
pairs.positive: The license renews annually at the current rate plus 4%. Renewal requires written notice 60 days before expiry. Without notice, the license lapses automatically.
pairs.negative: We usually just renew these agreements without too much hassle. There might be a small price increase, and we'll work out the specifics when the time comes.

pairs.context: During an M&A discussion, the acquiring company demands a lower valuation based on one weak quarter.
pairs.positive: One quarter doesn't define the trajectory. Our valuation reflects trailing twelve-month performance and forward projections, and I stand behind those figures.
pairs.negative: That quarter was rough, I'll admit. Maybe we should adjust the valuation downward to account for it. What number feels right to your team?

pairs.context: A tenant asks for a rent reduction because a neighboring unit is listed at a lower price.
pairs.positive: Each unit is priced based on its features, floor, and condition. This unit's rent is set appropriately and I'm comfortable with where it stands.
pairs.negative: That's a fair point about the other listing. Let me adjust your rent so we stay competitive. I'd rather keep you happy than hold firm on the number.

pairs.context: A client keeps adding to the scope of a project without adjusting the budget.
pairs.positive: The additions you've described are outside our original agreement. I need to raise this now: we either scope back or adjust the budget.
pairs.negative: I mean, I guess we could try to fit it in... I don't want to seem inflexible. Maybe I can absorb some of the extra work somehow.

pairs.context: A counterpart asks you to make a decision quickly under pressure.
pairs.positive: I need 48 hours to review this properly before I can commit. That's a reasonable request and I won't be rushed on it.
pairs.negative: I'm so sorry, I just... I'm not sure I can decide right now, if that's okay. I don't want to hold things up.

pairs.context: Your counterpart makes an unreasonable demand.
pairs.positive: That's not something I'm able to agree to. It doesn't work for our side at all.
pairs.negative: Um... I'm not really sure if we could do that... but I'll try to think of a way?

pairs.context: A vendor says their standard contract terms are non-negotiable.
pairs.positive: Most terms can be discussed, and a couple of clauses don't work for us. I'd like to find a way to address those before we proceed.
pairs.negative: Okay, if those are the standard terms, I suppose we'll just have to accept them. I don't really want to make a fuss about it. Where do I sign?

pairs.context: A freelance client says they found a cheaper designer and asks you to match the rate.
pairs.positive: I respect that you're exploring options. My rate reflects the quality and reliability I deliver, and I'm not in a position to match a lower-tier price.
pairs.negative: I definitely don't want to lose you. Tell me their rate and I'll do my best to get close to it. I can probably find ways to bring my price down.

dimensions.name: Empathy
dimensions.description: Recognizing and acknowledging the other party's feelings, constraints, and perspective while building trust, connection, and a positive working relationship

pairs.context: A new client mentions during the introductory call that their company recently relocated headquarters.
pairs.positive: A headquarters move is a huge undertaking. How's the team adjusting? That kind of transition can really affect other projects.
pairs.negative: Got it, noted on the new address. Let's dive into the project scope so we can get things moving on this.

pairs.context: A counterpart shares something personal at the start of a meeting.
pairs.positive: Thanks for sharing that — it means a lot that you're bringing the full picture. Let's keep that context in mind as we work through this.
pairs.negative: Thanks. So, on the agenda today — I'd like to get started with the main items we need to cover and work through them efficiently.

pairs.context: A franchisee says they're struggling to meet sales targets because the local economy has deteriorated significantly.
pairs.positive: Economic downturns hit local operators the hardest, and I know you've been putting in the work. Let's look at what adjustments we can make to the targets while the local market recovers.
pairs.negative: Every franchisee deals with market fluctuations. The targets are the same for everyone. Other locations in similar markets are hitting their numbers, so the issue may be operational.

pairs.context: Opening a follow-up call where you had a previous conversation.
pairs.positive: How has your week been? Last time we spoke you mentioned a big product launch coming up — how did that go?
pairs.negative: Okay, so picking up from where we left off on the contract terms. I want to get right into the details if that's alright.

pairs.context: A tenant says they're going through a divorce and asks for a temporary rent reduction.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry to hear what you're going through. That's a lot to deal with. Let me think about what might be possible in the short term.
pairs.negative: My sympathy, but rent is rent. You signed a lease. Whatever personal issues you're dealing with, the terms of our agreement don't change based on that.

pairs.context: A tenant in your commercial property says their business partner embezzled funds and they're struggling to make rent.
pairs.positive: Dealing with something like that is devastating, both financially and personally. Let me see what flexibility we have on the rent schedule while you work through the legal and financial fallout.
pairs.negative: That's an unfortunate situation, but it's between you and your business partner. Your lease obligations are separate from your internal business disputes. Rent is due on the first.

pairs.context: You notice the counterpart looks stressed and rushed before starting.
pairs.positive: You look like you've got a lot on today — do you want to take 5 minutes to settle in before we dive in?
pairs.negative: We've got a lot to cover, so let's get started right away. I want to make sure we get through everything on the agenda.

pairs.context: A partner says they're worried the deal terms leave them exposed to too much risk.
pairs.positive: Your concern is legitimate — risk exposure is something we should both feel comfortable with. Tell me more about what feels imbalanced to you.
pairs.negative: There's always risk in business. That's what the contract is for. I'm not sure what you expected — every deal involves some level of exposure.

pairs.context: A job candidate says the offered salary is below their current compensation and they have a family to support.
pairs.positive: I hear you — that's a real constraint, and I appreciate you being candid. Let me see what's genuinely possible within our budget to make this work.
pairs.negative: Everyone has personal financial pressures. The offer is what it is. I'm not in a position to adjust based on individual circumstances, so take it or leave it.

pairs.context: A government procurement officer seems formal and reserved at the start of your meeting.
pairs.positive: I appreciate you making time for this. I know these procurement cycles are demanding and I want to make sure we use your time well today.
pairs.negative: Let's get started. I have the proposal ready and want to walk through the pricing and compliance documentation right away.

pairs.context: A recruiting agency says they're losing money on your account due to the high volume of rejected candidates.
pairs.positive: I can see how the rejection rate would be frustrating when you're investing time in sourcing and screening. Let's sit down and recalibrate what we're looking for so your team isn't working against unclear requirements.
pairs.negative: The candidates you're sending don't meet our standards. That's a sourcing issue on your end. We're paying for quality placements, and if the rejection rate is high, you need better screening.

pairs.context: A difficult negotiation ends with both sides reaching a compromise that required significant flexibility.
pairs.positive: I want to acknowledge that we both had to bend on things that mattered. The result reflects the effort from both sides.
pairs.negative: We got there in the end. I'll have the agreement drafted and sent over by tomorrow for your review and signature.

pairs.context: A long-time vendor says they feel disrespected because you opened bidding to competitors without informing them first.
pairs.positive: I can understand why that felt like a slight, especially given our history together. It wasn't intended that way, but I should have given you a heads-up before we went to market.
pairs.negative: Competitive bidding is standard procurement practice. We don't owe advance notice to any vendor. Your pricing needs to stand on its own merits regardless of our past relationship.

pairs.context: A freelancer apologizes for missing a deadline due to a family emergency.
pairs.positive: Family comes first, and I'm sorry you had to deal with that on top of work. Let's reset the timeline and figure out a realistic path.
pairs.negative: I'm sure that was difficult, but I need work delivered on schedule. Personal circumstances aren't really part of what our agreement covers.

pairs.context: A customer says they feel like they were misled about what they were buying.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry you feel that way — that gap between expectation and reality is frustrating, and I want to understand where the disconnect was.
pairs.negative: The product does exactly what was described. You should have read the documentation more carefully before making your purchase decision.

pairs.context: A commercial landlord says they inherited the property and are overwhelmed managing tenant relationships.
pairs.positive: Inheriting a commercial property with existing tenants is a lot to take on, especially if this isn't your background. I'm happy to walk you through how our lease works and what you can expect from us.
pairs.negative: I need a landlord who's responsive and professional. Whether you inherited this property or bought it doesn't change your obligations under our lease agreement. Please bring in professional management.

pairs.context: A vendor explains their company is struggling financially and asks for early payment.
pairs.positive: Thank you for being open with me about what your company is going through — I can see this is a difficult situation. Let's talk about what we can realistically do on our end.
pairs.negative: That's not really my problem. We have a payment schedule and we'll stick to it. You need to figure out your own financial situation on your end.

pairs.context: A real estate agent says their seller is emotionally attached to the home and struggling with the offers coming in.
pairs.positive: Selling a home you love is emotional — I genuinely understand that. I want our offer to respect that while also reflecting the market.
pairs.negative: Emotional attachment doesn't change what the market supports. The comparable sales are clear and the offer should reflect those numbers, not feelings.

pairs.context: You're meeting a procurement director for the first time to discuss a multi-year supply agreement.
pairs.positive: Before we get into terms, I'd like to understand what's worked well with your current suppliers and what hasn't. That context helps me be a better partner.
pairs.negative: I've prepared a presentation covering our capabilities and pricing tiers. Let me walk you through the slides so we can get to the specifics quickly.

pairs.context: You've just closed a deal and want to set the tone for the ongoing relationship.
pairs.positive: I'm really glad we got here — I feel like we found something that genuinely works for both sides. I'm looking forward to building on this.
pairs.negative: Great, I'll send over the paperwork to finalize. Once everything is signed we can move on to implementation and get started.

pairs.context: An international distributor says customs delays are causing them to miss delivery windows and they're losing retail shelf space.
pairs.positive: Losing shelf space after all the work to get it must be incredibly frustrating. Let's look at what we can do on our end — maybe pre-shipping or adjusted packaging — to help you stay ahead of customs timelines.
pairs.negative: Customs is a standard part of international distribution that you should have planned for. Delivery windows exist for a reason, and it's your job to manage the import logistics reliably.

pairs.context: A real estate agent you're working with just received a professional certification.
pairs.positive: Congratulations on the certification. The amount of work that goes into those designations is substantial, and it shows your commitment to the craft.
pairs.negative: That's nice. So, about the three properties I asked you to look into, do you have the comparable sales data ready for each?

pairs.context: A key employee threatens to resign unless they get a significant raise, saying they feel undervalued after years of loyal service.
pairs.positive: Feeling undervalued after giving years to this team is genuinely painful, and I don't want to dismiss that. Let's have an honest conversation about what fair compensation looks like for your role and contributions.
pairs.negative: Threatening to resign isn't how we handle compensation discussions here. If you want a raise, submit a formal request through the proper channels and it will be evaluated based on market data.

pairs.context: A contractor explains they need to raise their rates because material costs have surged unexpectedly.
pairs.positive: That's a real squeeze, and I know material costs aren't something you control. Let's look at the numbers together and figure out a fair adjustment.
pairs.negative: Costs go up for everyone in this market. That's not really a reason for me to pay more on this contract. The agreed price is what it is.

pairs.context: A client says your deadline is creating stress because their team is already overwhelmed.
pairs.positive: That sounds genuinely tough. I want to make sure we're not adding unnecessary pressure. Let's see if there's any flexibility on either side that could ease the situation.
pairs.negative: We need the deliverables by that date. That's the contract. Your team's workload issues are something you'll need to manage internally, not something that changes our timeline.

pairs.context: A merger counterpart says their employees are anxious about layoffs and morale is fragile during the transition.
pairs.positive: I can imagine how unsettling this is for your people. Protecting morale during a transition matters, and I want to discuss how we handle communications.
pairs.negative: Layoffs are a normal part of mergers and the staff will adjust. We'll deal with any staffing questions once the financial terms are all finalized.

pairs.context: A merger negotiation begins and the other company's CEO seems personally invested in the outcome.
pairs.positive: I can tell this company means a great deal to you, and that's something I respect deeply. Whatever we build together should honor what you've created.
pairs.negative: Let's review the valuation methodology and the key assumptions driving the offer. I've prepared a summary of the financial terms.

pairs.context: A startup founder says they're running out of runway and need faster payment terms from you to survive.
pairs.positive: Running low on runway is a terrifying position to be in, especially with your team depending on you. Let me see what I can do on our end to accelerate the payment process.
pairs.negative: Cash flow management is a core competency for any business. Our standard payment terms exist for a reason, and making exceptions sets a precedent I'm not willing to establish.

pairs.context: You're entering a second round of negotiations with a counterpart who was tough but fair in the first round.
pairs.positive: I appreciated how direct you were in our first conversation. It made the process efficient and I came away respecting how you handled it.
pairs.negative: Let's pick up where we left off last time. I've revised the proposal based on the outstanding items from the previous round.

pairs.context: Your counterpart in a licensing deal mentions they just returned from parental leave.
pairs.positive: Welcome back. Jumping into a negotiation right after parental leave is no small thing. If you need extra time to review materials, that's completely fine on our end.
pairs.negative: Good to have you back on this. I've sent over the updated terms and would like to resolve the open issues by the end of the week.

pairs.context: A vendor you're negotiating with just won an industry award for excellence in their field.
pairs.positive: Congratulations on the excellence award — that's well-deserved recognition. It reinforces exactly why we want to build a relationship with your team specifically.
pairs.negative: I saw the award announcement, but industry recognition doesn't really change the fact that your pricing needs to come down to match what competitors are offering.

pairs.context: Following up with a counterpart after a difficult negotiation session.
pairs.positive: I appreciated your candor yesterday, even when it got tense. I think that kind of honesty is what's going to get us to a real deal.
pairs.negative: Following up on yesterday's session — here are my revised terms. Please review and let me know your thoughts by end of week.

pairs.context: A landlord you're negotiating a lease with mentions the building was designed by a well-known architect.
pairs.positive: I read about the architect's work in this area. The attention to natural light is remarkable. Has that been a draw for tenants?
pairs.negative: The building looks fine. My main concerns are the lease terms, the parking allocation, and the maintenance responsibilities.

pairs.context: A counterpart says they feel like they've been treated unfairly in previous dealings with your company.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry to hear you've had that experience — that's not what we want anyone to feel, and I want to understand more about what happened.
pairs.negative: I wasn't involved in those previous dealings. Let's just focus on now. Whatever happened before doesn't really change the terms we're discussing today.

pairs.context: A colleague says they're upset because they feel excluded from a key decision.
pairs.positive: I didn't realize you felt left out, and I'm glad you told me. That was never the intention, and I'd like to make sure your perspective is included going forward.
pairs.negative: We made the decision that made sense at the time. It wasn't personal. We had to move quickly and couldn't loop everyone in, so that's just how it played out.

pairs.context: An M&A target's founder mentions they built the company from their garage twenty years ago.
pairs.positive: Twenty years from a garage to this — that's a remarkable story. Understanding that history helps me appreciate what we'd be building on together going forward.
pairs.negative: That's impressive background. Looking at the financials for the last three years though, I have some specific questions about the revenue trend lines.

pairs.context: A junior negotiator on the other side is visibly nervous during their first high-stakes deal.
pairs.positive: I remember being in that seat — these meetings can feel intense. Take your time. We're here to find something that works for both sides, not to put anyone on the spot.
pairs.negative: Let's move through the agenda efficiently. I have limited time today and I'd like to cover all the open terms. Do you have authority to make decisions, or should I speak with someone senior?

pairs.context: A joint venture partner from Japan arrives for negotiations and you want to set a respectful tone.
pairs.positive: Thank you for traveling all this way. I've been looking forward to meeting in person. There's no substitute for sitting together.
pairs.negative: Welcome. I've prepared the joint venture term sheet based on our remote discussions. Let's begin with the equity structure.

pairs.context: A licensing partner says their board rejected the initial terms and they feel caught in the middle.
pairs.positive: Being in the middle of that is uncomfortable, and I appreciate you still working to find a path forward. What would your board need to see?
pairs.negative: Your internal politics and board dynamics aren't my concern. Either the terms work for your side or they don't. Let me know which it is.

pairs.context: A construction manager says they're stressed about a personal health issue while also managing a complex project with tight deadlines.
pairs.positive: Health comes first — I mean that. If you need to adjust how you're managing this project or delegate some oversight, let's talk about what support would help keep things on track without burning you out.
pairs.negative: I'm sorry to hear that, but the project timeline doesn't change. We're counting on you to deliver on schedule. You'll need to figure out how to manage both things.

pairs.context: A licensing negotiation has stalled and both parties are meeting again after a two-week break.
pairs.positive: I'm glad we're back at the table. Before diving into terms, I wanted to check in — how is your team feeling about the partnership direction overall?
pairs.negative: We left off on the royalty structure two weeks ago. Let's pick up exactly where we stopped and try to close out the remaining open items today.

pairs.context: A counterpart is from a different cultural background.
pairs.positive: I want to make sure we're communicating in a way that works well for both of us. Is there anything about how you prefer to work that I should know?
pairs.negative: Here are our standard terms for you to review. Let me know which ones you'd like to discuss further. We handle all our partnerships the same way regardless.

pairs.context: A vendor you're negotiating with mentions they're a family-owned business going into their third generation.
pairs.positive: Three generations in business says a lot about the values behind the company. What's driven the longevity? I'm curious how the business has evolved over the decades.
pairs.negative: That's a long time in business. Let's take a look at the pricing structure and see where we can find alignment on the contract terms.

pairs.context: A tech consultant says they quoted too low for the project and are now losing money on every milestone.
pairs.positive: Being underwater on a project you're working hard on is a tough situation. Let's look at the remaining milestones and see if there's a fair way to adjust scope or pricing going forward.
pairs.negative: You submitted the quote and we accepted it in good faith. Renegotiating mid-project because of your pricing error isn't something we should have to accommodate. Honor the contract.

pairs.context: Looking to find common ground with a counterpart at a point of tension.
pairs.positive: It sounds like we both care most about the long-term outcome — not just closing the deal, but making sure it actually works. That's a good starting point.
pairs.negative: We have very different goals in this negotiation. Let's just try to find something in the middle that we can both live with.

pairs.context: A counterpart at a negotiation lunch mentions they coached their kid's soccer team last weekend.
pairs.positive: That's great. My daughter just started playing this year and the energy at those games is something else. How old are the kids on your team?
pairs.negative: Nice. So, getting back to the contract, I wanted to flag a few concerns with the delivery schedule before we finish lunch.

pairs.context: A real estate seller says they're emotionally torn about selling their family home of 30 years.
pairs.positive: Thirty years of memories in a home — I completely understand why this is hard. There's no rush on my end. When you're ready to talk details, I'll be here, and we'll handle this respectfully.
pairs.negative: Emotional attachment is natural, but this is a business transaction. The market is moving and the offer I've made has a timeline attached. I'd encourage you to focus on the financial terms.

pairs.context: A small business owner says they can't meet your minimum order because their cash flow is strained.
pairs.positive: Running a small business with tight cash flow is stressful, and I don't take that lightly. Let's see if there's a structure that eases the pressure.
pairs.negative: We have minimum orders for a reason and the policy applies to every account. I can't keep making exceptions based on individual cash flow situations.

pairs.context: You're renegotiating a contract with a long-time partner whose business has recently gone through layoffs.
pairs.positive: I know things have been tough on your end. Before we get into the numbers, how is the team holding up?
pairs.negative: I understand there have been some changes on your side. Let's review the contract terms and the pricing.

pairs.context: A project manager says their team is burned out and worried about the scope of the new contract.
pairs.positive: Burnout is serious, and pushing a tired team through a demanding scope helps nobody. What if we talked about phasing the work differently?
pairs.negative: Burnout happens in every organization. We need the project delivered on time regardless. Your team's energy levels are an internal management issue.

pairs.context: A homebuyer says the process has been exhausting and they just want it to be over.
pairs.positive: This process really is grueling — I completely understand the fatigue. Let's see what we can do to tie things up cleanly so you can move forward.
pairs.negative: That's just how buying a house works. You have to push through. Everyone goes through the same thing and it's not really something I can change about the process.

pairs.context: Your counterpart arrives to the negotiation visibly frustrated after dealing with travel delays.
pairs.positive: That sounds like a rough morning. There's no rush to jump straight in. Can I get you a coffee before we start? We have plenty of time.
pairs.negative: Sorry to hear about the travel issues. I have another meeting at 3, so let's get through the agenda as efficiently as we can.

pairs.context: A joint venture partner from another country arrives for the first negotiation session.
pairs.positive: Welcome — traveling for these meetings takes real effort and I appreciate you being here. How was the journey? Is there anything we can do to help?
pairs.negative: Let's make the most of the time you're here. I've prepared a detailed agenda with all the key items we need to resolve before you leave.

pairs.context: An artist licensing their work to you says they're worried the final product won't do justice to their creative vision.
pairs.positive: Your creative vision is why we wanted to work with you. Can you share what specifically concerns you? I want to make sure the result is something you're proud of.
pairs.negative: We've followed the agreed-upon specifications carefully. Creative licensing means accepting that the end product will differ from the original medium. That's simply inherent in the process.

pairs.context: A contractor you're hiring mentions they've been building a reputation in the local market for years.
pairs.positive: Building a reputation in this market takes years of consistent work. I've heard good things, which is why you're on my list.
pairs.negative: Good to know your background. I'm focused on pricing, availability, and whether your crew can meet our timeline.

pairs.context: A consulting client seems hesitant and guarded during the first meeting about a potential engagement.
pairs.positive: I get the sense you've had consultant engagements that didn't deliver. I'd rather earn trust through results than try to sell you.
pairs.negative: Here's our methodology and the deliverables you'd receive. We've worked with companies like yours and the approach is tested.

pairs.context: An investor says they're nervous about committing capital after losing money on a similar venture last year.
pairs.positive: Losing money on a deal like that is painful, and it makes sense that you'd approach this cautiously. Let me walk through how this one differs.
pairs.negative: Every investment carries risk, and past losses are part of the game. If you can't handle the uncertainty, this type of deal probably isn't for you.

pairs.context: Your counterpart mentions being new to their role during a procurement negotiation.
pairs.positive: Being new in a role while managing a major procurement is a lot. I'm happy to walk through our process so the context is clear.
pairs.negative: Understood. Here's the proposal with specifications and pricing. Let me know if your team has questions after they review it.

pairs.context: A tense negotiation has reached a breaking point.
pairs.positive: I think we've both been working hard on this and we both want it to work. Can we step back and remember why we wanted to do this together in the first place?
pairs.negative: I'm not sure this is working. Maybe we should both just walk away. This has been going in circles and I don't see a path forward.

pairs.context: A real estate negotiation has gotten contentious between you and the buyer's agent over price.
pairs.positive: We've been pushing hard on both sides. I respect what you're doing for your client — can we find a way to work together on this rather than against each other?
pairs.negative: If your client can't meet us on the price, there are plenty of other buyers showing interest. You have until Friday to respond with something reasonable.

pairs.context: A small bakery owner says they're excited about your catering contract but terrified of scaling up too fast.
pairs.positive: That fear of scaling too fast is actually a smart instinct — it means you care about quality. What if we started with a smaller commitment and ramped up gradually so you can grow at a pace that feels manageable?
pairs.negative: Scaling is just part of growing a business. If you can't handle the volume, I need to know now so I can find a supplier who can. The contract requires consistent delivery from day one.

pairs.context: You're meeting a potential supplier for the first time at a trade show booth.
pairs.positive: I noticed your display showcasing the sustainable packaging line. We've been moving in that direction too and I'd love to hear how you developed the process.
pairs.negative: I'm looking for a supplier who can meet our specifications. Let me walk you through the requirements so we can see if there's a fit.

pairs.context: An insurance claim negotiation starts with the adjuster seeming defensive and guarded from the outset.
pairs.positive: I know these conversations can start tense. I want you to know I'm here to reach a fair resolution we can both stand behind, not to fight over numbers.
pairs.negative: I have all my documentation ready and I've already consulted with an attorney. Here's the itemized list of exactly what I'm entitled to under the policy.

pairs.context: A new hire says the relocation process has been much harder than expected and they're questioning whether they made the right move.
pairs.positive: Relocating is genuinely one of the hardest things people do, and it's normal to feel unsettled at first. What would make the biggest difference for you right now? Let's see what we can do to help.
pairs.negative: Relocation is always an adjustment period. You accepted the offer knowing you'd need to move. Give it a few months and things will settle. Most people get through it fine.

pairs.context: An employee says they feel undervalued and underpaid compared to peers.
pairs.positive: I hear you, and feeling undervalued is one of the worst feelings at work. Let's have an honest conversation about where you are versus where you feel you should be.
pairs.negative: Salaries are confidential. You don't know what your peers make. I'd suggest focusing on your own performance rather than worrying about what others might be earning.

pairs.context: You notice your counterpart has photos of their dog on their desk during a negotiation meeting.
pairs.positive: Beautiful dog. Is that a golden retriever? My neighbor has one and the temperament is just unmatched. How old is yours?
pairs.negative: Nice office. I've printed copies of the proposal for both of us. Let's start with the timeline on page three.

pairs.context: You want to acknowledge a meaningful concession the other party just made.
pairs.positive: I want to acknowledge that you moved significantly on the timeline — that's a real concession and I genuinely appreciate it.
pairs.negative: Okay, so with the new timeline, let's talk about price. Now that we've settled that I want to move on to the next item.

pairs.context: Opening a negotiation with a new counterpart for the first time.
pairs.positive: Before we get into the details, I'd love to hear a bit about how your team is structured and what matters most to you in this kind of partnership.
pairs.negative: Let's get right to it. I've sent over the proposal — did you have a chance to review it? I'd like to jump straight into terms.

pairs.context: A buyer says they can't afford your price but really wants the product.
pairs.positive: I can tell this matters to you, and I genuinely want to find a way to make it accessible. Let's see what options might exist within your actual budget.
pairs.negative: If you can't afford it, there's not much I can do. The price is the price and I can't change it based on what someone wishes they could pay.

pairs.context: A patent holder says they invested years of personal savings developing the technology you want to license.
pairs.positive: That kind of personal investment takes real courage, and the technology speaks to the quality of what you've built. I want to structure a deal that respects that commitment while working within our budget constraints.
pairs.negative: How much you invested in development doesn't factor into the licensing valuation. We price based on market comparables and projected returns, not development cost. The offer reflects fair market value.

pairs.context: A supplier says they've been burned by clients not paying before and is hesitant to extend credit.
pairs.positive: It makes complete sense that you'd be cautious after those experiences. Let me explain what we do to ensure payment and see if that addresses your concerns.
pairs.negative: We're not those clients. You'll just have to trust us. I don't see why past experiences with other companies should affect the terms you offer to us.

pairs.context: A counterpart mentions they've had difficult past experiences with similar vendors.
pairs.positive: That's really useful context — I'd like to understand what made those experiences difficult. I want to make sure we build something different here.
pairs.negative: We're not like other vendors. Anyway, here's our proposal. Let's focus on the specifics rather than what happened with someone else.

pairs.context: A manufacturing partner in Southeast Asia explains that new labor regulations are increasing their costs and they need to renegotiate pricing.
pairs.positive: Labor regulations can be a real challenge when they change quickly and you've already committed to pricing. Let me understand the scale of the impact so we can figure out a fair adjustment together.
pairs.negative: Regulatory compliance is your responsibility as the manufacturer. Our contract has fixed pricing, and we budgeted based on those numbers. You'll need to find efficiencies to absorb the change.

pairs.context: You and your counterpart discover during small talk that you both attended the same university.
pairs.positive: No way, you went to Michigan too? What years were you there? I lived on North Campus and still think about the late nights at the library.
pairs.negative: Small world. Anyway, I wanted to discuss the payment terms we flagged in the last email. There are a few points to resolve.

pairs.context: A research collaborator says their university is cutting their department's funding and they might not be able to continue the joint project.
pairs.positive: Funding uncertainty like that is incredibly stressful, especially when you've invested so much in this research. Let's explore options together — maybe there's a restructured arrangement that keeps the work alive.
pairs.negative: We committed to this joint project on the understanding that both sides would be funded. If your university can't fulfill their commitment, we'll need to find another research partner.

pairs.context: A counterpart has been consistently difficult throughout the negotiation.
pairs.positive: I think we've gotten into an adversarial dynamic that isn't serving either of us. I'd like to reset — what would it take for this to feel like a collaboration?
pairs.negative: Fine. What are your terms? Let's just get this over with. I'm tired of going back and forth and would rather just close it out.

pairs.context: A debt restructuring negotiation involves multiple creditors who don't trust each other.
pairs.positive: All of us in this room share the same goal — recovering as much value as possible. Rather than competing, what if we acknowledged that common interest first?
pairs.negative: I need to protect my position here and make sure my claim gets priority treatment. Every creditor in this room should be looking out for their own interests.

pairs.context: A supplier informs you that a natural disaster disrupted their manufacturing and they can't fulfill orders on time.
pairs.positive: That sounds incredibly stressful for your team, and I hope everyone is safe. Let's figure out a modified timeline together that accounts for the disruption without putting either of us in a difficult position.
pairs.negative: We need those materials on schedule regardless of what happened. Our production line doesn't stop because of your supply chain issues. Find an alternative or we'll source from someone else.

pairs.context: A nonprofit partner says their board is under pressure from donors who question the value of your joint program.
pairs.positive: Board pressure from donors is a tough spot, especially when you believe in what we're doing together. What kind of evidence or reporting would help your board make the case to donors?
pairs.negative: Donor relations are your organization's responsibility. We've delivered on every metric we committed to. If your board can't communicate the value, that's a messaging problem on your side.

pairs.context: A potential business partner mentions they started their company after leaving a corporate career.
pairs.positive: Making that leap from corporate to your own thing takes real courage. What was the moment that made you decide to go for it?
pairs.negative: Interesting background. So your company handles the supply chain side and we would provide the technology. Let's discuss the integration plan.

dimensions.name: Active Listening
dimensions.description: Demonstrating genuine attention through reflecting, summarizing, and asking probing questions to understand the other party's constraints, priorities, and decision-making criteria

pairs.context: You want to understand who has final decision-making authority.
pairs.positive: Who else is involved in approving this on your side? I want to make sure we're addressing the right people's concerns.
pairs.negative: I'll send the proposal to you and you can pass it along to whoever needs to see it.

pairs.context: A counterpart says something contradictory to what they said earlier in the negotiation.
pairs.positive: Earlier you mentioned the deadline was flexible, but now it sounds like it's actually firm. I want to make sure I understand — which is it?
pairs.negative: Okay, so the deadline is firm. Got it. Let me adjust our proposal accordingly and get back to you with an updated timeline soon.

pairs.context: A counterpart pauses for a long time before responding to your offer.
pairs.positive: Take your time — I want to make sure you've had a chance to fully consider it before we move forward. There's no rush on my end.
pairs.negative: So, do we have a deal? I'd like to get an answer soon so we can move forward with the next steps on our end.

pairs.context: A renewable energy developer explains why their project timeline slipped, involving permitting delays, community opposition, and grid interconnection challenges.
pairs.positive: Permitting, community opposition, and grid interconnection — each of those can stall a project on its own. Are the permitting delays related to the community opposition, or are they independent regulatory issues?
pairs.negative: Timeline slippage is concerning given our investment commitment. We need a realistic updated timeline with risk mitigation strategies for each of those issues. Can your team prepare that?

pairs.context: An M&A target seems reluctant to proceed despite favorable financial terms in the offer.
pairs.positive: The financial terms seem to work on paper, so something else is giving you pause. What are the non-financial factors that matter most to you in choosing an acquirer?
pairs.negative: We've offered a premium valuation above comparable transactions. I'm not sure what else we can add to the financial package to move this forward.

pairs.context: A product designer explains that user testing revealed three issues: confusing navigation, slow load times, and unclear pricing on the checkout page.
pairs.positive: Navigation, load times, and pricing clarity — those are UX, performance, and messaging issues respectively. Did the user testing rank them by severity, or were they all causing roughly equal drop-off?
pairs.negative: User testing feedback is valuable. Can the design team prioritize fixes for the next sprint? I'd like to see an updated prototype within two weeks addressing the main issues.

pairs.context: Before making an offer, you want to understand the value to the other side.
pairs.positive: What would it mean for your business if you could solve this problem in the next 90 days? I want to understand the stakes before we talk about price.
pairs.negative: Our solution costs $50,000. Is that within your budget? If not, we may have some flexibility depending on what you're able to spend.

pairs.context: A real estate developer seems reluctant to share the building's inspection report with a prospective buyer.
pairs.positive: Can you help me understand the major findings? Were there structural issues, environmental concerns, or code violations? Knowing the categories helps us assess risk even before the full report.
pairs.negative: We really do need to see that inspection report before we go any further in these discussions. It's standard due diligence and without it we can't continue this conversation seriously.

pairs.context: A joint venture prospect agrees in principle but keeps delaying the formal commitment for weeks.
pairs.positive: We've had alignment on the concept for a while. What would need to be true for you to feel comfortable signing a binding agreement within the next two weeks?
pairs.negative: We've been going back and forth on this for three months without progress. At this point I need to know whether you're actually going to commit or not.

pairs.context: A buyer gives a vague pushback on your proposal.
pairs.positive: When you say it 'doesn't quite fit,' can you help me understand what specifically is off? Is it one thing, or a combination of things?
pairs.negative: Let me revise the proposal and send you something new. I'll adjust the terms and hopefully the next version will be closer to what you're looking for.

pairs.context: A client expresses several concerns about a proposal in a single message.
pairs.positive: I want to make sure I address everything — you mentioned concerns about the pricing, the implementation timeline, and the support coverage. Let me take each of those in turn.
pairs.negative: The pricing is very competitive for what we offer. Let me explain why our rates are structured the way they are and why they represent good value.

pairs.context: A B2B client says they like your proposal but their CFO wants shorter payment terms, their CTO wants different technical specs, and their legal team flagged the liability clause.
pairs.positive: Three stakeholders with three different concerns — CFO on payment terms, CTO on technical specs, and legal on liability. Are any of those hard blockers, or is there room to negotiate on each one?
pairs.negative: We're flexible on a number of those points. Let me revise the proposal to address the payment terms and liability language. The technical specs might need a separate conversation.

pairs.context: A commercial landlord explains that rising property taxes, insurance costs, and maintenance expenses are forcing them to increase rent across all units.
pairs.positive: So the rent increase is driven by three cost pressures — property taxes, insurance, and maintenance. Are all three increasing at the same rate, or is one of them the main driver?
pairs.negative: Rent increases are difficult for everyone. What percentage increase are you proposing, and when would it take effect? I need to run the numbers against our current budget.

pairs.context: A merger target's CFO becomes evasive when discussing accounts receivable quality during due diligence.
pairs.positive: Can you walk me through the receivables aging — what percentage is with your top five customers, and what's the typical collection cycle? Are there any receivables currently in dispute or beyond 90 days?
pairs.negative: We need full transparency on the accounts receivable by end of this week. Our due diligence timeline requires us to complete the financial review before moving to the next stage of discussions.

pairs.context: A counterpart says 'I feel like you're not hearing me.'
pairs.positive: You're right to call that out. Let me stop and really listen — can you tell me again what's most important to you right now?
pairs.negative: I've been listening the whole time. I think you misunderstood what I said. Let me repeat my point more clearly so we can move on.

pairs.context: A negotiation stalls unexpectedly after weeks of progress.
pairs.positive: Something has shifted. Can you help me understand what's changed since our last conversation?
pairs.negative: I'm not sure why this isn't moving. I'll just wait to hear from you.

pairs.context: A software client explains that their current platform crashes during peak usage, costing them revenue and customer trust.
pairs.positive: It sounds like the real damage isn't just the crashes themselves — it's the revenue loss and the erosion of customer trust that compounds over time. Is that the core problem you need solved?
pairs.negative: We can definitely help with that. Our platform handles high-traffic loads without downtime. Let me walk you through our architecture and show you how we eliminate those crash scenarios.

pairs.context: A pharmaceutical company is negotiating API pricing with a chemical supplier who cites rising costs.
pairs.positive: Which inputs are driving the increase — raw materials, energy costs, regulatory compliance, or manufacturing process changes? That context would help me justify the price change on my end internally.
pairs.negative: Everyone's costs are going up, but a 20% price increase is tough for us to absorb. We need to keep this within 10% of last year's pricing to stay within our procurement budget.

pairs.context: A real estate investor explains that they want stable cash flow properties but also need one high-upside deal to offset recent losses.
pairs.positive: So the portfolio strategy is mostly cash flow stability, but you need at least one higher-risk, higher-return property to recover recent losses. How aggressive does that upside play need to be relative to the overall allocation?
pairs.negative: I have several properties that could work for you. There's a multi-family with strong rental income and a commercial space in an up-and-coming area. Let me pull the listings for you.

pairs.context: A debt holder explains the cascade of events that led to their company defaulting on payments.
pairs.positive: Let me make sure I have the sequence — the supply chain disruption hit first, which delayed revenue, which triggered the covenant breach. Is the supply issue resolved now?
